critic_raw:
This is a well-structured experimental design with genuine attention to leakage, temporal validity, and operational grounding. I'll work through the issues I can identify by root cause.

---

**Root Cause 1: The relevance label encodes the model's own prior predictions and team bandwidth artifacts (action-label bias — acknowledged but underspecified)**

The methodology acknowledges 'action-label bias' but treats it as a caveat rather than a structural threat to validity. The specific mechanism deserves sharper treatment.

The claim is that 'remediated within 21 days or escalated via IR' is a decision-proximal proxy for true exploitability-relevant priority.

Why this might be wrong: The remediation label is generated by the same security team whose queue ordering is currently determined by CVSS. Findings at the top of the CVSS-sorted queue are patched first — not because they are more exploitable, but because they were surfaced first. Findings buried below rank 100 in the CVSS baseline are almost never touched within 21 days simply due to bandwidth, not because they are irrelevant. This means the training labels are systematically missing positive examples for findings that CVSS underranks. When the LTR model is trained on these labels and then evaluated on NDCG@100, it is being rewarded for learning to reproduce the existing CVSS-induced selection process, not for discovering genuinely better prioritization signal. The ablation showing 'severity-only' versus full-feature models will not cleanly separate this confound because the label itself is severity-contaminated.

A secondary mechanism: IR escalation events are rare and concentrated on specific asset classes (internet-exposed, high-criticality), which are also the strata in the proposed ablations. The stratified results will look stronger for those segments not because the model generalizes better there, but because the label density is higher there due to analyst attention patterns.

What constitutes evidence one way or the other: Compare the positive label rate for findings that appear in ranks 1–100 of the CVSS baseline versus findings in ranks 101–500. If the positive rate drops sharply below rank 100 independently of any other feature, that is strong evidence of bandwidth-induced label truncation rather than true signal. A secondary check: examine whether the 21-day window was calibrated to match the team's actual sprint/patch cycle, or whether it was chosen because it produces a useful label prevalence. If the window is tightly matched to the patch cycle, the label is measuring 'did this get scheduled' rather than 'was this important.'

---

**Root Cause 2: NDCG@100 evaluation against a CVSS baseline conflates two hypotheses that need to be separated**

The claim is that NDCG@100 improvement over CVSS demonstrates that combining inventory, topology, and scanner signals improves operational prioritization.

Why this might be wrong: NDCG@100 is computed against labels that, as argued above, have a high base rate at the top of the CVSS ranking. A model that learns CVSS as a feature and then uses asset criticality to break ties within the top CVSS band will mechanically improve NDCG@100 over CVSS alone — because it is refining the ordering within a stratum that already contains most of the positive labels. This improvement does not demonstrate that topology or inventory signals are adding useful prioritization beyond CVSS; it demonstrates that the label distribution is concentrated where CVSS already points, and that secondary sorting within that band is easily learned. The specific ablation design (severity-only, asset-only, asset+exposure) partially addresses this, but only if those ablations are evaluated on a label set that is not itself severity-contaminated. On the current labels, the severity-only ablation will be a surprisingly strong competitor, making the marginal gain from topology appear modest — or the topology signal will appear to add value simply by memorizing which asset classes get patched first.

What constitutes evidence one way or the other: If the severity-only ablation achieves NDCG@100 within a few points of the full model, and the full model's gain is concentrated in the internet-exposed/high-criticality stratum, this is consistent with the confound. To partially disentangle, hold out a subset of weeks where the team's patch capacity was constrained (e.g., weeks with large vulnerability disclosure events that flooded the queue), because in those weeks the label truncation effect is most extreme and a model relying on genuine signal should degrade relative to normal weeks more than a CVSS-mimic would.

---

**Root Cause 3: Graph feature recomputation is described but the leakage surface is underspecified**

The claim is that graph features are recomputed per week from network data available at that point, preventing temporal leakage.

Why this might be wrong: Network topology summaries like centrality scores are computed over the observed graph at time T. But the graph at time T reflects which assets have already been segmented, firewalled, or decommissioned in response to prior incidents. Assets that were central in earlier weeks may no longer appear central after a remediation campaign. If those remediation campaigns were triggered by the findings being studied, then the graph state at scoring time partially encodes downstream remediation decisions, not just the static network topology. This is a subtler leakage than the temporal split catches, because it operates within the per-week feature construction rather than across the train/test boundary.

Additionally, CMDB attributes (asset criticality classifications, owner assignments) are frequently back-filled or corrected after incidents. If the CMDB snapshot used for feature construction reflects the state of the CMDB at data extraction time rather than at the scoring week, criticality labels for assets involved in past incidents will be inflated at training time.

What constitutes evidence one way or the other: Check the distribution of CMDB 'last_modified' timestamps relative to the scoring week for each training example. If a nontrivial fraction of CMDB records were modified after the scoring week they are attributed to (because the historical snapshot was pulled from current CMDB rather than versioned snapshots), the asset-criticality features are leaking future information. For the graph leakage, compare centrality distributions for assets with a remediation event in week T versus those without — if remediated assets show systematically lower centrality in week T than in week T-4, the graph reflects remediation history rather than independent topology.

---

**Root Cause 4: The logistic regression baseline may not be a fair non-ranking comparator**

The claim is that logistic regression on tabular non-graph signals with comparable hyperparameter tuning effort establishes whether gains come from ranking formulation and nonlinear interactions.

Why this might be wrong: The comparison is described as 'non-graph signals' for logistic regression versus 'graph signals included' for the LTR model. If graph signals are the primary differentiator and they are withheld from the logistic regression baseline, then an improvement over that baseline conflates two factors: (1) ranking formulation versus classification, and (2) graph features versus no graph features. The methodology says this baseline 'establishes whether any gain comes from the richer ranking formulation and nonlinear interactions rather than from simply adding obvious metadata' — but if graph features are not 'obvious metadata' and are only given to the LTR model, the baseline does not isolate the ranking formulation question.

What constitutes evidence one way or the other: Add a gradient-boosted classifier (not LTR) on the full feature set including graph features, scored by probability descending, evaluated with the same NDCG@100 metric. If that classifier matches the LTR model closely, the gain is from features, not ranking formulation. If the LTR model still wins substantially, the within-query structure is doing real work.

---

**Summary of Verdict**

The action-label bias issue is the most structurally damaging because it threatens the validity of the evaluation, not just the interpretation of results. The CVSS-contaminated labels mean the experiment is well-positioned to confirm that CVSS-correlated signals are useful, but poorly positioned to demonstrate that topology or inventory features are adding genuine prioritization value beyond reproducing existing analyst behavior. The other issues are real but partially addressable within the described design. The core action-label contamination issue cannot be resolved without either (a) a parallel human-judgment relevance assessment on a sample of findings that were never acted on, or (b) a natural experiment exploiting exogenous variation in what the team could observe (e.g., a queue flooding event or a tool outage that randomized what got reviewed). Without one of these, the experiment establishes 'our model replicates and marginally improves on historical analyst decisions' rather than 'our model prioritizes genuinely higher-risk findings.'
all_issues_raised: Relevance labels are generated by the existing CVSS-ranked workflow, so findings below analyst bandwidth cutoffs are systematically missing positive labels regardless of true risk, causing the LTR model to learn to replicate CVSS-induced selection rather than genuine exploitability signal.

NDCG@100 improvement over CVSS is structurally likely because positive labels concentrate in the top CVSS band, so any model that refines ordering within that band will appear to win without demonstrating topology or inventory signals add genuine value.

CMDB attributes may reflect back-filled post-incident classifications rather than contemporaneous state if versioned snapshots were not preserved, leaking future criticality assignments into training features.

Graph centrality features may encode prior remediation history if network segmentation decisions following incidents reduce centrality of already-remediated assets, making topology appear predictive when it is partly an artifact of past actions.

The logistic regression baseline excludes graph features while the LTR model includes them, so improvements over that baseline cannot be attributed to ranking formulation alone — a gradient-boosted classifier on the full feature set is the missing ablation.
all_issues_adjudicated: Relevance labels are generated by the existing CVSS-ranked workflow, so findings below analyst bandwidth cutoffs are systematically missing positive labels regardless of true risk, causing the LTR model to learn to replicate CVSS-induced selection rather than genuine exploitability signal.

NDCG@100 improvement over CVSS is structurally likely because positive labels concentrate in the top CVSS band, so any model that refines ordering within that band will appear to win without demonstrating topology or inventory signals add genuine value.

CMDB attributes may reflect back-filled post-incident classifications rather than contemporaneous state if versioned snapshots were not preserved, leaking future criticality assignments into training features.

Graph centrality features may encode prior remediation history if network segmentation decisions following incidents reduce centrality of already-remediated assets, making topology appear predictive when it is partly an artifact of past actions.

The logistic regression baseline excludes graph features while the LTR model includes them, so improvements over that baseline cannot be attributed to ranking formulation alone — a gradient-boosted classifier on the full feature set is the missing ablation.
